s2.q: Which of the following is a cause of onset of normal labor?
s2.explain: Onset of labor is associated with ↑ Estrogen, ↓ Progesterone, ↑ Prostaglandin F2α and ↑ Oxytocin, leading to uterine contractions. Mechanical factors (uterine distension) also contribute.

s2.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s2.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s2.q: Which of the following best defines this condition?
options: A pathological deviation from normal anatomy or function
An incidental finding with no clinical significance
s2.explain: A definition describes the essential nature of a condition as a deviation from normal.

s2.q: The incidence of this condition is highest in which population?
options: Depends on the specific condition and risk factors
s2.explain: Incidence varies by condition — each has its own epidemiology and risk profile.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s3.q: Prelabor (premonitory stage) begins how long before true labor in primigravida?
s3.explain: Prelabor begins 2-3 weeks before true labor in primigravida and a few days before in multipara. It is associated with increased oxytocin receptors.

s3.q: Which of the following is NOT a change seen during prelabor?
s3.explain: Changes during prelabor include lightening, cervical ripening, and false labor pains. The bag of water forms during true labor, not prelabor.

s3.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s3.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

It has been replaced by newer classifications

s3.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings

s4.q: Which feature distinguishes true labor pains from false labor pains?
options: Pain location is only lower abdomen
s4.explain: True labor pains produce cervical changes (dilatation and effacement), while false labor pains do not. True contractions are regular and progressive, and show (blood-stained mucus) is present.

s5.q: The first stage of labor ends with:
s5.explain: The first stage starts with onset of true labor pain and ends with full dilatation of cervix (10 cm). Duration is 12 hours in primi and 6 hours in multipara.

s5.q: The third stage of labor normally lasts about:
s5.explain: The third stage (from fetal expulsion to placental expulsion) lasts about 15 minutes in both primi and multipara with expectant management, reduced to 5 minutes with active management.

s6.q: The pacemaker of uterine contractions is situated at:
s6.explain: The pacemaker of uterine contractions is at the cornu, with the right pacemaker predominating over the left. Contractions spread at 2 cm/sec, depolarizing the whole organ within 15 seconds.

s6.q: A Montevideo unit measures uterine contractions as:
options: Intensity × number of contractions in 3 minutes
s6.explain: Montevideo unit (MV unit) = Intensity of uterine contraction × number of contractions in 3 minutes. It quantifies uterine activity.

s7.q: In a normal labor, cephalic presentation occurs in what percentage of cases?
s7.explain: Cephalic presentation occurs in 95% of labors, breech in 3.5%, and shoulder in 0.5%.

s7.q: The normal cervical dilatation rate in active phase for nulliparous women is:
s7.explain: In the active phase, normal cervical dilatation rate is 1.2 cm/hr for primigravida and 1.5 cm/hr for multiparous women.

s7.q: The latent stage of first stage is mainly concerned with:
s7.explain: The latent stage is mainly concerned with cervical effacement, while the active stage is mainly concerned with cervical dilatation.

s7.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s7.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s8.q: A prolonged latent phase is defined as greater than how many hours in nulliparous women?
s8.explain: Prolonged latent phase is >20 hours in primigravida and >14 hours in multipara. Management includes a therapeutic test with morphine or oxytocin stimulation.

s8.q: Arrest of dilatation is defined as cessation of dilatation for:
s8.explain: Arrest of dilatation is cessation of dilatation for 2 or more hours. Arrest of descent is cessation of descent for 1 or more hours.

s8.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s8.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s9.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the cardinal movements of the mechanism of labor?
s9.explain: The cardinal movements are: descent, engagement, increased flexion, internal rotation, extension, restitution, and external rotation. Cervical dilatation is not a fetal movement — it occurs due to uterine contractions.

s9.q: Engagement of the vertex is diagnosed vaginally when:
options: The vertex is felt at or below the ischial spine (zero station)
s9.explain: Engagement is diagnosed vaginally when the vertex is at or below the ischial spine (zero station). Abdominally, 2/5 or less of the head is felt above the symphysis pubis.

s10.q: The active management of the third stage reduces its duration to:
s10.explain: Active management reduces the third stage duration from 15 minutes (expectant) to 5 minutes, lowering the risk of PPH and maternal mortality.

s10.q: Which of the following is a sign of placental separation?
options: Uterus becomes globular, hard, and mobile with a sudden gush of blood
s10.explain: Signs include: uterus becomes globular/hard/mobile, sudden gush of blood, suprapubic bulge, rise of fundal level, lengthening of cord, and absence of cord pulsation.

s11.q: Which of the following is NOT part of the active management of the first stage?
s11.explain: Active first-stage procedures include evacuation of rectum/bladder, analgesia, partogram, amniotomy, oxytocin augmentation, and fetal monitoring. Fundal pressure is not recommended.

options: Depends on the specific condition and severity
s11.explain: Management approach varies — some conditions require surgery, others medical or conservative care.
Choice of investigation depends on the suspected condition.

s12.q: Cord clamping is contraindicated in which of the following?
s12.explain: Cord clamping is contraindicated in preterm babies and Rh-negative mothers. The newborn care also includes airway clearance, APGAR scoring, examination, and eye care with antibiotics.
